steps.title: Test the marketing motive
steps.content: The student's claim is not just that explanatory notes were added to the stand-alone volume. The claim is that the notes were included primarily as a marketing device to help sell that volume. Strong support therefore needs evidence about the publishers' purpose: the notes had to be requested or included to make the republished poem more attractive to potential buyers, especially readers who might otherwise see no reason to buy a poem they already had in journal form.

steps.title: Separate motive from effects
steps.content: Choice A says the poem's text changed between publications, but changes to the poem itself do not explain why notes were included. Choice B reports critics' views that the notes made the poem less interesting, which concerns later literary effects, not the reason for adding them. Choice D says the notes went through several drafts, but drafting history does not show a sales motive. Choice C directly says the publishers requested the notes to make the book attractive to readers who already owned the journal version, so it supplies the commercial motive the claim needs. The answer is C.
